1.pericope_label: The Fifth Trumpet: The Locusts from the Abyss
1.exposition: With the fifth trumpet the judgments change register. The first four struck the natural order — earth, sea, rivers, sky; now a shaft is opened into the underworld itself, and the terror becomes spiritual and demonic. A star fallen from heaven is handed the key of the abyss, and when the pit is unsealed the smoke of a great furnace darkens sun and air (v.2). Out of the smoke swarm locusts armed with the sting of scorpions, forbidden to touch grass or tree and loosed only against those who lack the seal of God. They may torment but not kill; for five months men seek death and cannot find it. Their king is the angel of the pit, named Abaddon, Apollyon — the Destroyer. The composite monster — human faces, women's hair, lions' teeth, iron mail — defies any literal zoology and demands to be read as a symbol; but a symbol of what? The historicist tradition heard the Saracen armies swarming out of Arabia; the idealist, the spread of heresy and demonic assault through the whole age; the futurist, a still-future plague loosed from the pit. What the witnesses hold in common is the frame: the abyss is opened only by a key that is given, and the woe is bounded to five months.
1.original_language: The pit is the abyssos (the "bottomless" place, alpha-privative + bythos, depth), reached through a phrear — a well or cistern with a narrow mouth (v.1); the same abyssos the demons dreaded in Luke 8:31. The star is seen peptōkota (perfect participle, "already fallen," not in the act of falling), which is why the older versions' "fall" misleads — John sees a being already down. The creatures are akrides ("locusts"), but their work is basanizō, to "torment" as on a touchstone (v.5) — pain, not devastation. The king's name is given in two tongues to underline its meaning: Hebrew Abaddon ("destruction," cf. Job) and Greek Apollyōn ("Destroyer") — one office under two names (v.11).
1.historical_context: Every feature of the swarm is quarried from the prophets. The locust is the proverbial devouring host — the eighth plague of Egypt (Exodus 10) and, above all, the army of Joel 2, whose locusts run "like horses," with "the teeth of a lion" (Joel 1:6) and a noise "like chariots" (Joel 2:5) — precisely the composite of vv.7–9. The fallen star recalls the "Day Star, son of Dawn" hurled down in Isaiah 14:12, and anticipates the dragon cast to earth in 12:9. The five months answer to the natural life-span of a locust brood, marking the plague as bounded and seasonal, not final. Some note an anti-imperial edge: Domitian courted identification with Apollo, and the pit's king bears "Apollo's" destroying name.
1.christ: The whole vision hangs on a passive verb: the key "was given" (v.1). Abaddon is a prisoner of the pit, not its lord; the pit is unlocked from outside, and the one who truly holds "the keys of Death and Hades" is the risen Christ (1:18). The five-month leash (v.5) and the ban on killing are the christological constraint: hell's forces cannot cross the boundary the Lamb has set. And the seal of God on the forehead (v.4; cf. 7:3) proves effective against the Destroyer's authority — those who bear the Lamb's mark the locusts cannot touch.
1.judgment: This is a judgment strictly targeted: the locusts pass over the sealed and fall only on "the men who have not the seal of God" (v.4), the earth-dwellers who have worshipped the beast. Its horror is that it does not kill but prolongs — a torment from which even death, longed for, flees (v.6). It is the exact inverse of the martyrs' lot: they die and are vindicated (6:11), while the impenitent live and cannot escape. Yet even this woe is measured and merciful in scope — a warning trumpet, not the last word, closing as "one woe" with two still to come (v.12).

verses.note: John sees a star fallen from heaven, to whom "was given the key of the bottomless pit." grammatical-historical Robertson stresses the perfect participle — the star is already down, a personal being, not a meteor, for a key is given to "him." Its identity divides the witnesses: puritan Matthew Henry reads an apostate minister turned "the devil's turnkey," loosing hell against the churches (a historicist strand named Boniface); latin-fathers Bede reads the old enemy himself, who "fell like lightning" (Luke 10:18), opening human hearts by false doctrine. Ellicott notes the emblem is Satanic — a fallen, not falling, star, like the Day Star of Isaiah 14:12. Either way the key is given: the pit opens only by leave of the Christ who holds its keys (1:18).

The shaft opened, smoke rose as from a great furnace, darkening sun and air. grammatical-historical Barnes hears the language of Sodom, whose ruin "went up as the smoke of a furnace" (Genesis 19:28) — the plague appears as if vented from hell itself. latin-fathers Bede and wesleyan-arminian Wesley take the smoke as false and idolatrous doctrine, a spreading darkness that obscures even the light of the lofty. Ellicott: the pit's smoke is the diffusion on earth of "evil thoughts and ideas," hostility to truth — light not extinguished but hindered.

Out of the smoke came locusts with the power of scorpions. The picture is drawn from Egypt (Exodus 10) and Joel, where locusts image a vast devouring army. But their identity is the great crux. grammatical-historical Barnes, reading the Oriental symbol, and reformed Gill both look east — Gill to the Romish clergy and, in the wider application, to Mahomet's Saracens; the historicist school hears an invading host out of Arabia. latin-fathers Bede reads spiritually: the locusts are heretics, members of the dragon's body who "hurt with the mouth." A futurist strand takes them as a literal, future demonic plague. Ellicott presses what unites these readings: locusts given a scorpion's sting are plainly no natural insects but "a malicious energy."

Against all locust-nature, they are forbidden the grass and the trees and sent only against those without the seal of God. grammatical-historical Barnes marks the reversal — their war is on men, not vegetation, which alone proves them no ordinary swarm; Robertson, with Swete, sees the Exodus pattern: "as Israel in Egypt escaped the plagues, so the new Israel is exempt." latin-fathers Bede takes the very fact that they strike men, not grass, as proof they signify men. The sealed (7:3) are untouchable — an echo of Christ's word that nothing shall hurt those given power over scorpions (Luke 10:19).

The plague is doubly bounded: not to kill, but to torment five months, with pain "as of a scorpion when it strikes." grammatical-historical Barnes weighs the number — either the literal locust-season of about five months, or, on the prophetic day-for-a-year reckoning, some hundred and fifty years (the historicist computation) — but insists the definite "five" forbids reading it merely as "a short time." The torment (basanizō) is acute yet non-lethal. wesleyan-arminian Wesley: few were killed; rather they were "imprisoned and variously tormented."

So unbearable is the sting that men will seek death and not find it. grammatical-historical Robertson, following Swete, presses the point: no physical death can cure "the torment of an evil conscience," so death is desired but denied. The language is Job's — those who "long for death, but it cometh not, and dig for it more than for hid treasures" (Job 3:21). Ellicott hears the note of every "reign of terror": life becomes a mockery to the miserable, and death, once feared, flees.

The description now builds the war-picture: shapes like horses arrayed for battle, golden crowns, human faces — straight from Joel 2:4, "their appearance is as the appearance of horses." grammatical-historical Barnes reads a disciplined cavalry; the "crowns like gold" a counterfeit of conquest (the historicist sees the turbans of the Arabs), and the "faces of men" a mark of intelligence — a reasoning host, not mere insects. The composite is deliberately impossible, forcing the reader past the literal to the symbol.

They had hair as of women and teeth as of lions. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the long, flowing hair adds to the wild and terrible aspect (some historicists heard the long hair of the Arabs), while the lions' teeth are lifted verbatim from Joel 1:6 — a nation "whose teeth are the teeth of a lion," the emblem of a devouring ferocity. puritan Matthew Henry reads the allurement morally: the tenderness of women's hair masking the cruelty of a lion's bite.

Breastplates as of iron, and wings that roared like chariots rushing to war — again Joel 2:5, "like the noise of chariots on the tops of mountains." grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the iron mail as invulnerability and martial array (the historicist, the armor of an invading cavalry), and the thunder of the wings as the terror of an advancing army whose very sound unnerves. puritan Matthew Henry: they "made a mighty noise in the world," flying from country to country.

The sting returns: tails like scorpions, and power to hurt men five months. grammatical-historical Barnes locates the whole hurt in the tail — and in the historicist reading, a corrupting doctrine left poisoning the wake of the invasion. puritan Matthew Henry: after flattering with a fair appearance, "there was a sting in their tail," a cup luscious at first that at length "would bite like a serpent." The repeated "five months" (v.5) again fixes the plague as bounded.

Unlike natural locusts, which "have no king" (Proverbs 30:27), these are ruled: the angel of the abyss, Abaddon in Hebrew, Apollyon in Greek — both names meaning Destroyer. puritan Matthew Henry: an angel still by nature, but a fallen one, a "prince and governor" over the powers of darkness whose whole business is to destroy. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott take him for Satan or a satanic angel, the presiding spirit of ruin (the historicist, the false prophet at the head of the host). The name is his character: he leads a destroying power — yet a chained one, whose destroying is licensed and limited.

The structuring refrain: "One woe is past; behold, two woes are still to come." grammatical-historical Barnes recalls that the last three trumpets were announced as three "woes" (8:13): the locust-torment being the first, the Euphratean cavalry (the sixth trumpet) and the seventh remain. The narrative carefully marks the sequence, and heightens the dread — the worst is not yet over.

witnesses.point: Reads the fallen star as the old enemy who fell like lightning, and the locusts as heretics — members of the dragon's body who hurt with the mouth and carry poison in the tail; the sealed cannot be touched.
The Oriental locust-symbol, given a scorpion's sting and forbidden to harm vegetation, marks no natural swarm but a tormenting host from the abyss; the five months are a bounded, definite period.

The plague is not military but spiritual — a secret poison in the soul robbing men of purity and peace; God's distinguishing grace keeps the sealed from total apostasy, and the Destroyer's power is limited in time.
The star is already fallen (perfect participle); as Israel escaped Egypt's plagues, the new Israel is exempt from the locusts, for no physical death can remedy the torment of a guilty conscience.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Reads the locusts as demonic forces afflicting unbelievers — a spiritual torment permitted throughout the church age — and hears in "Apollyon" a pointed irony against Apollo, the god the imperial cult prized; the demonic is unleashed, but only under God's sovereign permission.

1.application: The one defense in this vision is not strength but a seal — the mark of belonging to the Lamb (v.4). The saints are not spared every affliction, but the assault of the pit cannot finally reach those who are Christ's. When the air darkens with dread, the question is not whether you can fight the locusts, but whose mark you bear.

13.pericope_label: The Sixth Trumpet: The Army from the Euphrates
13.exposition: The sixth trumpet is the second woe, and it is deadlier than the first: the locusts could only torment, but now a third of mankind is slain. The command issues from the four horns of the golden altar — the altar of incense where the prayers of the saints ascend (cf. 8:3), so that this judgment answers the martyrs' cry, "How long?" Four angels bound at the great river Euphrates are loosed at an exactly appointed hour, day, month, and year, and behind them rides a cavalry of two hundred million, their lion-headed horses breathing fire and smoke and brimstone. Historicists read the Ottoman Turks with their new artillery; preterists, the dreaded Parthian horse-archers beyond Rome's eastern frontier; idealists, the demonic armies of judgment loosed across the age. Yet the vision's most devastating stroke is its last: the survivors did not repent. A third of the world dies, and the rest go on worshipping idols of gold and silver, unrepentant of their murders and sorceries. The trumpet that kills a third cannot soften the hearts of the other two-thirds — and that hard fact prepares the way for the bowls.
13.original_language: The cavalry is numbered dismyriades myriadōn — "twice ten-thousand times ten-thousand," two hundred million (v.16): not a census but a figure for a host beyond counting, no army that ever marched. The four angels are "loosed" (lyō) — released from restraint (v.14), held ready "for the hour and day and month and year," the piling of time-units stressing an appointment fixed to the moment. The catalogue of unrepented sins climaxes in pharmakeia (v.21) — "sorceries," the magic arts bound up with drugs and the occult — and the whole is framed by the twice-sounded verb that carries the passage's weight: they "did not repent" (ou metenoēsan, vv.20–21).
13.historical_context: For a Roman reader the Euphrates was the edge of the world — the empire's eastern frontier, beyond which lay Parthia, Rome's one unconquered dread, whose mounted archers had annihilated legions and whose invasion was a standing nightmare (the "Nero returning with the kings of the East" legend fed the fear). To loose angels bound there is to unleash the terror from the East. The horses' threefold breath — fire, smoke, and brimstone (v.17) — is the ruin of Sodom (Genesis 19:24) and the covenant curse of a scorched land (Deuteronomy 29:23). The historicist tradition, hearing "fire and smoke" from the mouths, read the gunpowder and cannon of the Ottoman armies. The idol-list of v.20 — gold, silver, bronze, stone, wood — reproduces the prophets' mockery of dead gods (Psalm 115:4–7; Daniel 5:23).
13.christ: The judgment's clock is not the invaders' but God's: the angels are prepared "for the hour and the day and the month and the year" (v.15) — an appointment as precise as the "hour" Christ names for his own cross (cf. John 2:4). Nothing here is random or out of hand. And the command comes from the golden altar (v.13), the very place where the saints' prayers were offered (8:3): the Lamb who received the martyrs' cry of "how long" (6:10) now answers it. Even the armies of the Euphrates serve the intercession of his people.
13.judgment: Here the trumpets reach their most sobering disclosure. A third of humanity is killed (v.18), yet "the rest of mankind" — those spared — did not repent of the works of their hands. They cling to their idols and to murder, sorcery, immorality, and theft (v.21). The plague that meant to be a summons is met with the hardness of Pharaoh, on whom plague after plague fell without softening the heart. The lesson is severe: judgment by itself does not convert. Wrath can terrify but cannot renew; repentance is a gift of grace, not a reflex of fear. This impenitence is why the trumpets give way, at last, to the bowls (ch. 16) — no longer warnings, but the outpoured end.

verses.note: The sixth angel sounds, and a voice comes from the four horns of the golden altar before God. grammatical-historical Barnes marks the source: the altar of incense (8:3), where the prayers of the saints are offered. puritan Matthew Henry draws the connection out — the command for judgment issues from the place of the church's prayers, so that God's avenging acts answer the cries of his persecuted people (6:10). The next woe is not blind fate but the throne responding to intercession.

The voice bids the trumpet-angel loose the four angels bound at the great river Euphrates. latin-fathers Victorinus reads the four as the angels of four nations across the river, restrained until at the last "they shall come with Antichrist" — held to their appointed bounds. grammatical-historical Barnes takes them as agents of judgment kept back till the set time (the historicist, the powers behind the Ottoman invasion long held in check). The Euphrates was the great eastern boundary, and to loose what is bound there is to release the terror of the East.

The angels are loosed, prepared for the hour and day and month and year, to kill a third of mankind. grammatical-historical Barnes hears in the heaped time-units the stress on an exactly fixed moment — nothing left to chance — while noting the historicist attempt to compute the units as a prophetic period. Unlike the locusts, who only tormented, this woe is lethal: a "third" marks a vast but still limited destruction, a judgment more severe yet not the final end.

John hears the number of the cavalry: two hundred million — "twice ten thousand times ten thousand." grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read a host so vast as to be beyond ordinary reckoning; a definite symbolic number for an uncountable multitude (the historicist, the swarming armies of the East). "I heard the number of them": the immensity is not vague but declared, a host past all human count yet precisely known to God.

The vision itself: riders in breastplates of fire, hyacinth, and sulphur; horses with lions' heads; and from their mouths fire and smoke and brimstone. grammatical-historical Barnes matches the three colors of the mail — fiery red, dusky blue, sulphurous yellow — to the three deadly issues from the mouths, a hellish array; the lion-heads mark ferocity and destruction. reformed Gill and Adam Clarke, reading the fire and smoke from the mouths, heard the artillery and firearms of the invading host. The image is drawn from Sodom's overthrow (Genesis 19:24).

By these three plagues — fire, smoke, brimstone — a third of mankind was killed. grammatical-historical Barnes names them distinct instruments of the slaughter, the deadly commission of v.15 carried out; the second woe brings death, not mere torment. The threefold ruin is the covenant curse of a land laid waste with "brimstone and salt and burning" (Deuteronomy 29:23) — severe, extensive, yet bounded to a third: a warning, not yet the consummation.

Their deadliness is twofold: their power is in their mouths and in their tails, for the tails are like serpents, with heads that wound. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the mouth as the killing fire already described (vv.17–18) and the serpent-tail as a lasting, poisonous power left in the wake — the host lethal front and rear. The serpent-tail recalls the sting of the fifth trumpet's locusts, binding the two woes together as a single demonic assault.

The hinge of the whole passage: the rest, not killed by these plagues, did not repent of the works of their hands — they would not stop worshipping demons and idols of gold, silver, bronze, stone, and wood, "which can neither see nor hear nor walk." grammatical-historical Barnes finds nothing more strikingly shows idolatry's grip on the mind: plague after plague falls, and still they cling to their idols — the very hardening of Pharaoh. The mockery of senseless gods is the prophets' own (Psalm 115:4–7; Daniel 5:23).

The catalogue completes the indictment: neither did they repent of their murders, their sorceries, their sexual immorality, or their thefts. reformed Gill notes the survivors did not reform even from their murders — "the shedding of the blood of the saints" — nor from their pharmakeia, the magic and occult arts. The twice-sounded "did not repent" is the point: judgment alone, however deadly, does not soften the heart. The heart must be changed by grace, not merely terrified by wrath — which is why the trumpets must give way to the bowls.

witnesses.point: Reads the four angels of the Euphrates as the restrained angels of four nations beyond the river, held to their appointed bounds until at the last they come with Antichrist — the loosing is a release of long-restrained powers at God's set time.

The heaped time-units mark a judgment fixed to the exact moment; the two hundred million is a symbol of a countless host; and the survivors' impenitence shows nothing more powerfully than idolatry's grip on the human mind.

The fire and smoke from the horses' mouths he read as the artillery of the invading East; and the unrepented murders as the shed blood of the saints — the impenitent reforming from none of their vices under judgment.

The command for the sixth-trumpet judgment comes from the golden altar of prayer, binding God's avenging acts to the cries of his persecuted church; the judgment answers the saints, though it does not convert the world.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.work: The Theology of the Book of Revelation
external.point: Sees the trumpet-plagues patterned on the plagues of Egypt — warnings meant to bring the nations to repentance — and reads vv.20–21 as their tragic failure: unlike Pharaoh's Egypt, the world will not repent under judgment alone; only the sacrificial witness of the church (ch. 11), not sheer catastrophe, leads the nations to give God glory.

13.application: It is a myth that suffering, by itself, makes people good. A third of the world falls, and the survivors bury their dead and return to their idols. Judgment can expose the heart; it cannot renew it. If even plague will not turn us, then repentance is not something wrung out of us by fear but a mercy given — and the door still open before the bowls is the door of grace, not of terror.

_meta.title: The Fifth and Sixth Trumpets: The Woes from the Pit and the East
reflection: The locusts cannot touch "those who have the seal of God on their foreheads" (v.4). What does it change to know that your safety in spiritual assault rests not on your strength but on whose mark you bear?
The command for judgment rises from the altar where the saints' prayers are kept (v.13; cf. 6:10). How does it reframe your prayers for justice to know they are heard and, in God's time, answered?

After a third of the world dies, the survivors still "did not repent" (vv.20–21). Where have you assumed that hardship alone would soften a hard heart — your own or another's — when what is needed is the gift of grace?
